card.title: How Pompeii Was Frozen in Time
card.teaser: A city of 20,000 vanished in 18 hours — and was preserved so perfectly that loaves of bread still sit in the ovens.
card.coverImagePrompt: Mount Vesuvius erupting behind the ruins of Pompeii at twilight, columns still standing, ash-laden wind bending cypress trees, dramatic cinematic light

lessons.title: A city eating lunch when the sky turned black
lessons.body: In the autumn of 79 CE, Pompeii was a thriving Roman resort town of roughly 20,000 people. Bakers were pulling bread from ovens. A dog was chained in a courtyard. A family was finishing eggs and figs. Then Mount Vesuvius exploded with the force of 100,000 Hiroshima bombs — and everyday life stopped mid-bite.

visual.imagePrompt: A bustling Roman street market, sky darkening overhead as ash begins to fall on terracotta rooftops

lessons.title: Two different killers
lessons.body: Vesuvius didn't kill Pompeii with lava. First came an 18-hour hailstorm of pumice and ash, piling 9 feet deep and collapsing roofs. Then — around sunrise the next morning — pyroclastic surges arrived: waves of 250°C gas and ash moving at 450 mph. Nobody outdoors survived them. They killed in seconds.

keyTerms.definition: Light volcanic rock full of gas bubbles, rained down in the first phase
A fast-moving cloud of hot gas and ash from a volcanic eruption

visual.imagePrompt: Diagram of a pyroclastic surge racing down Vesuvius toward Pompeii at dawn, glowing orange beneath black clouds

lessons.title: Hollow ghosts in the ash
lessons.body: As bodies decomposed inside the hardened ash, they left human-shaped voids behind. In 1863, archaeologist Giuseppe Fiorelli had a brilliant idea: pour liquid plaster into the cavities. When it dried, the ash was chipped away — revealing perfect casts of the dead, frozen in their final postures. A mother shielding her child. Two lovers embracing. A dog writhing against its leash.
lessons.funFact: More than 100 casts have been made; modern versions use translucent epoxy so scientists can see the bones inside.

visual.imagePrompt: Plaster cast of a Pompeii victim curled on their side, soft museum lighting on ashen texture

lessons.title: A time capsule of Roman life
lessons.body: Because Pompeii was buried so fast and so deep, we have things that shouldn't survive anywhere else: carbonized bread with the baker's stamp, graffiti insulting politicians, frescoes with paint still vivid, brothel menus, fish-sauce factories, political campaign posters. Pompeii is not mostly a city of the dead — it's a city of the everyday.

visual.imagePrompt: A wall of ancient graffiti scratched into red plaster, Latin letters catching raking sunlight

lessons.title: The volcano that gave us Rome
lessons.body: Nearly everything we know about ordinary Roman life — what they ate, joked about, complained about — comes from Pompeii and its sister town Herculaneum. The disaster was catastrophic for the people of 79 CE. For historians, it's the closest thing to a live broadcast from the ancient world. And Vesuvius is still active.

quiz.question: What actually killed most of Pompeii's residents?
options: Pyroclastic surges of hot gas and ash
quiz.explanation: Pyroclastic surges — 250°C clouds moving at highway speeds — killed everyone caught outside within seconds.

quiz.question: How did Giuseppe Fiorelli create the famous casts of victims?
options: He poured plaster into voids left in the ash
quiz.explanation: In 1863, Fiorelli poured plaster into the cavities left by decomposed bodies, capturing their exact final postures.

quiz.question: What year did Vesuvius bury Pompeii?
quiz.explanation: The eruption occurred in autumn 79 CE, burying Pompeii and Herculaneum in pumice and ash.

sources.title: Pompeii: The Life of a Roman Town
The AD 79 eruption at Mt. Vesuvius: a review of the last 300 years of research
